Other Reason for Automation Desire: I’d like AI to assist—not automate—my instructional design, improving efficiency while preserving quality. It could organize SME content, suggest approaches for complex concepts, and draft initial documentation for refinement. This would reduce repetitive tasks, letting me prioritize strategic decisions, creative problem-solving, and human-centered design. AI wouldn’t replace me but transform my role to emphasize higher-value work over basic content structuring.
Other Reason for Human Agency: My input is vital because instructional design goes beyond organizing content—it requires strategic decisions aligning with learning goals and audience needs. I bring expertise in adult learning, scaffolding complex concepts, and anticipating struggles. While AI can draft and structure, it misses organizational context and nuance. My judgment ensures accuracy, accessibility, and creative, human-centered design that makes learning effective, not just informative.

Task: Evaluate modes of training delivery, such as in-person or virtual, to optimize training effectiveness, training costs, or environmental impacts.
Other Reason for Automation Desire: I’d like AI to analyze learning outcomes, costs, and environmental impacts across training delivery modes, providing data-driven insights and comparison matrices from large datasets and benchmarks. I’d make final strategic decisions, factoring in culture, preferences, and constraints AI can’t judge. AI handles the heavy data lift; I interpret it contextually, blending analysis with my instructional expertise for smarter delivery choices.
Other Reason for Human Agency: My input is crucial for evaluating training delivery modes beyond data analysis. While AI can crunch metrics and costs, I interpret them within organizational contexts—culture, stakeholder priorities, and constraints. My experience anticipates resistance, ensures learning transfer, and balances budget, effectiveness, and readiness. I make strategic, nuanced decisions AI can’t, drawing on years of instructional design wisdom across diverse settings.

Task: Monitor, evaluate, or record training activities or program effectiveness.
Other Reason for Automation Desire: I’d like AI to monitor training effectiveness by analyzing completion rates, scores, time metrics, and learner feedback across courses. It could create dashboards with trends and correlations, revealing what works best for different goals and groups. AI handles data crunching; I interpret patterns, contextualize insights, and refine designs—turning evaluation into ongoing, actionable improvements without the manual grind.
Other Reason for Human Agency: My input is essential for evaluating training effectiveness beyond data processing. I define metrics aligning with goals, interpret results contextually—e.g., when 70% completion is success or not—and spot strategy-outcome links AI might miss. I turn insights into practical design fixes, factoring in organizational dynamics and stakeholder needs AI can’t grasp, ensuring meaningful, actionable improvements.

Task: Design, plan, organize, or direct orientation and training programs for employees or customers.
Other Reason for Human Agency: My input is vital for designing training because it demands strategic vision and human-centered thinking AI can’t match. I uncover true business needs, tailor experiences to organizational cultures, balance priorities like depth versus efficiency, and craft authentic scenarios. My judgment integrates politics, culture, and realities AI can’t see, ensuring training aligns with outcomes and resonates with learners.

Task: Develop alternative training methods if expected improvements are not seen.

I interpret data contextually, gather nuanced feedback, and match strategies to challenges, drawing on experience AI can’t replicate. My contextual insight ensures viable, sustainable solutions tailored to organizational constraints and cultures.

Other Reason for Automation Desire: I’d like AI to evaluate training materials by checking readability, terminology consistency, objective alignment, and accessibility standards. Other Reason for Human Agency: My input is essential for evaluating training materials beyond standardized checks. I assess scaffolding for learner needs, spot missed opportunities for deeper understanding, and catch cultural or contextual issues AI might overlook. My experience ensures tone, detail, and examples fit the audience, drawing on practical judgment that exceeds codified criteria.

This frees me from admin work to focus on strategic resource decisions and explaining investment impacts, while AI ensures accuracy and consistency.
